Title: Isoprenoid metabolism and plastid reorganization in arbuscular mycorrhizal roots

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: AM root cortical cells; vacuolar lipophilic droplets
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DXS2-RNAi-mediated reduction of apocarotenoids was paralleled by >80% reduction of MtPT4 transcript, indicating reduced functional arbuscules rather than MtPT4 directly controlling metabolite accumulation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MtPT4 transcript abundance dropped by more than 80% in DXS2-RNAi transgenic roots with strongly reduced apocarotenoid levels. 2. MtPT4 is used as a marker for physiologically active phosphate-acquiring arbuscules. 3. Its response links apocarotenoid pathway activity with AM functionality, but MtPT4 is not itself a primary apocarotenoid metabolism gene.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi of DXS2 in transgenic roots; RT-PCR/transcript analysis of MtPT4; arbuscule frequency estimation
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it is experimentally connected to the metabolic phenotype in the review, but it functions mainly as an AM marker/transport gene, not as a biosynthetic or regulatory core apocarotenoid gene.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: expressed in physiologically active phosphate-acquiring arbuscules
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: expressed in physiologically active phosphate-acquiring arbuscules
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced expression associates with fewer functional arbuscules under reduced apocarotenoid production.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a functional readout when engineering apocarotenoid/AM pathways, but not a direct engineering target for increasing apocarotenoid accumulation.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: putative roles in AM symbiosis, colonization control, oxidative protection

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: plastidial MEP pathway for primary isoprenoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses pyruvate and glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate to form 1-deoxy-D-xylulose 5-phosphate.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies IPP precursor pool for carotenoid and apocarotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key upstream entry step, but not AM-inducible isoform
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: putative signaling, colonization control, oxidative protection in AM roots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DXS1 is constitutively expressed in photosynthetic tissues and is not the AM-induced isoform associated with apocarotenoid accumulation in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Medicago truncatula has two DXS isogenes, DXS1 and DXS2. 2. DXS1 is expressed at high constitutive levels in photosynthetic tissues. 3. DXS1 is associated with primary metabolism rather than AM-induced root apocarotenoid formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene expression analysis in Medicago truncatula
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: high constitutive expression in photosynthetic tissues
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: high constitutive expression in photosynthetic tissues
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: AM colonization distinguishes DXS2 from DXS1
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Less relevant target for AM-root apocarotenoid engineering than DXS2.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulating DXS1 may perturb primary plastid isoprenoid metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review proposes division of labor between DXS1 for primary metabolism and DXS2 for secondary/is stress-related metabolism.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: plastidial MEP pathway for secondary isoprenoid/apocarotenoid precursor supply
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses pyruvate and glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate to form 1-deoxy-D-xylulose 5-phosphate, initiating the plastidial MEP pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream; first committed entry step of MEP pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies IPP precursor pool for carotenoid formation leading to AM-specific apocarotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: supported as key control step for AM-root apocarotenoid production
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: putative signaling, control of fungal colonization, oxidative protection, promotion of symbiosis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: AM root cortical cells; vacuolar lipophilic droplets/yellow complexes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AM-induced DXS2 promotes apocarotenoid accumulation and functional arbuscule formation; DXS2-RNAi reduces apocarotenoids to ~10% of control, decreases MtPT4 transcripts by >80%, and lowers arbuscule frequency by ~50%.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXS2 is specifically induced upon AM colonization in Medicago truncatula roots. 2. It is proposed to provide MEP-derived precursors for secondary isoprenoid metabolism, including apocarotenoid production. 3. RNAi knockdown drastically reduced apocarotenoid levels, MtPT4 expression, and arbuscule abundance, linking DXS2 causally to AM functionality.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: expression analysis; Agrobacterium rhizogenes-mediated DXS2-RNAi in transgenic roots; metabolite profiling; MtPT4 transcript analysis; arbuscule frequency staining
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong engineering target for modulating AM-associated apocarotenoid metabolism and symbiotic performance, though manipulation may affect broader MEP-derived metabolites.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduction impairs functional arbuscules and symbiosis; broader effects on other MEP-derived isoprenoids are possible.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review suggests DXS2-like specialization for secondary metabolism may be widespread in plants. Applied species and source species are both Medicago truncatula for RNAi evidence, while comparable isoform division was also noted in maize and tomato.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Medicago truncatula; Zea mays; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Medicago truncatula; Zea mays; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Medicago truncatula; Zea mays; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Medicago truncatula; Zea mays; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts 1-deoxy-D-xylulose 5-phosphate to 2-C-methyl-D-erythritol 4-phosphate in the plastidial MEP pathway.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies MEP-pathway intermediates for carotenoid synthesis and downstream apocarotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key upstream pathway enzyme; induced in AM roots
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: putative signaling, colonization control, oxidative protection
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: AM root cortical cells; near senescent arbuscules
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DXR transcripts and protein increase in AM roots and localize predominantly to elongated plastids around old/senescent arbuscules, correlating with apocarotenoid accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXR transcript levels are increased in AM roots. 2. DXR protein accumulates in elongated/tubular plastids surrounding old or senescent arbuscules. 3. This spatial-temporal pattern links MEP-pathway activation with plastid reorganization and late-stage apocarotenoid formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transcript analysis; immunolocalization by confocal microscopy; plastid marker colocalization
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: upregulated in AM roots; strongest around mature/senescent arbuscules
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: upregulated in AM roots; strongest around mature/senescent arbuscules
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: arbuscular mycorrhizal colonization; arbuscule developmental stage
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to alter AM-specific isoprenoid flux, but likely with pleiotropic effects on plastidial isoprenoids.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbation may alter broad plastid metabolism and AM function.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes correlation of DXR accumulation with plastid elongation and apocarotenoid buildup in late colonization stages.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: electronic Northern; real-time PCR; metabolite profiling

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Nicotiana tabacum; Zea mays; various dicot species
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Nicotiana tabacum; Zea mays; Centaurea cyanus; Petroselinum crispum; Papaver somniferum; Tagetes erecta; Ruta graveolens
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes oxidation/desaturation of phytoene to ζ-carotene.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms carotenoid precursor pool for oxidative cleavage into mycorradicin and cyclohexenone derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: supported as key control point for AM-root apocarotenoid formation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: putative signaling and protective roles in AM roots
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: AM root cortical cells; vacuolar lipophilic droplets/soluble glycosides
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AM colonization increases PDS transcript/promoter activity; norflurazon inhibition causes phytoene accumulation and reduced mycorradicin, demonstrating carotenoid biosynthesis is required for apocarotenoid accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PDS expression is induced in AM roots of tobacco. 2. Promoter-GUS assays confirmed increased promoter activity in mycorrhizal roots. 3. Inhibition of PDS by norflurazon increased phytoene and reduced mycorradicin, causally linking carotenoid biosynthesis to AM-specific apocarotenoids. 4. Norflurazon-induced phytoene accumulation in several non-apocarotenoid-accumulating species indicates carotenoid pathway activation is a general AM response.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-PCR; promoter-GUS fusion analysis; inhibitor treatment with norflurazon; metabolite profiling of phytoene and mycorradicin
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important leverage point to manipulate AM-associated carotenoid flux, but inhibition reduces beneficial apocarotenoid formation and mycorrhization.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbing PDS may disrupt carotenoid homeostasis and impair colonization/symbiosis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper calls PDS a key enzyme of carotenoid biosynthesis and specifically notes it catalyzes the second committed step.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: PDS promoter-GUS fusion; inducible by AM colonization

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes desaturation steps leading from ζ-carotene toward lycopene.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms carotenoid precursor pool for oxidative cleavage to apocarotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: putative signaling and protective roles in AM roots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ZDS transcript levels are markedly increased in AM roots, supporting activation of carotenoid biosynthesis preceding apocarotenoid formation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZDS transcript levels increase in AM roots. 2. This induction supports enhanced carotenoid pathway activity in mycorrhizal roots. 3. ZDS acts upstream of the cleavage reactions generating AM-specific apocarotenoids.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary target for increasing pathway flux toward apocarotenoids.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering ZDS could broadly affect carotenoid metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review highlights ZDS induction but offers less direct functional perturbation evidence than for PDS or DXS2.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: oxidative cleavage of carotenoids at 9,10/9',10' positions
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: CCD family dioxygenase; lacks plastid transit peptide as CCD1-type
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: unknown carotenoid precursor, possibly lutein or lactucaxanthin
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: C13 ketones and C14 dialdehyde leading to cyclohexenone derivatives and mycorradicin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively cleaves a carotenoid at the 9,10 and 9',10' positions to generate C13 and C14 apocarotenoid fragments.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: branch/cleavage step generating terminal apocarotenoid backbones
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: supported as terminal determinant/decisive cleavage step, though actual substrate remains unknown
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: putative signaling, colonization control, oxidative protection
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: AM root cortical cells; vacuolar droplets/yellow complexes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: A mycorrhiza-inducible maize CCD1 homolog is strongly transcriptionally activated in AM roots and is proposed to catalyze the cleavage step generating the C13 and C14 apocarotenoid precursors of cyclohexenone derivatives and mycorradicin.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Strong transcriptional activation of a CCD occurs in AM maize roots. 2. The induced gene was identified as a CCD1 homolog (AY773278). 3. CCD1-type activity is proposed to cleave carotenoids at 9,10/9',10' to generate the direct precursors of AM-specific apocarotenoids. 4. The actual carotenoid substrate in AM roots remains unresolved.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transcript analysis; sequence homology/inference to CCD1; pathway reconstruction from metabolite structures
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: strongly induced in AM roots; high constitutive transcript levels also in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: strongly induced in AM roots; high constitutive transcript levels also in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Prime target for future RNAi/transgenic dissection of AM apocarotenoid synthesis and possible engineering of symbiotic performance.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Suppressing CCD1 may disrupt formation of biologically active apocarotenoids important for arbuscule function; actual substrate/product balance remains uncertain.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review explicitly proposes future CCD1-RNAi studies. The true substrate is unknown; lactucaxanthin is suggested as a candidate but not detected in roots.
